options: Veno-arterial (VA) ECMO may not be utilized in neonates with intracranial hemorrhage, while veno-venous (VV) may not
Veno-venous (VV) ECMO cannulation provides only pulmonary support
Veno-venous (VV) ECMO may result in complications of left ventricular distention
Veno-arterial (VA) ECMO cannulation provides only cardiac support
answer: Veno-arterial (VA) ECMO may not be utilized in neonates with intracranial hemorrhage, while veno-venous (VV) may not
rationale: Extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) may be necessary for the neonate with persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN) that is not improving with mechanical ventilation using high-frequency ventilation (oscillator, jet), inotropes, nitric oxide, and the administration of 100% oxygen. ECMO may be used to provide solely pulmonary support which is accomplished through veno-venous (VV) (also venous-venous) cannulation, or both pulmonary and cardiac support through veno-arterial (VA) (also venous-arterial) cannulation. All ECMO modalities require the patient to be heparinized, putting the patient at risk of bleeding; patients with major intracranial bleeding are not candidates for ECMO. ECMO may result in other complications, such as blood clot formation, infection, and transfusion problems, as well as mechanical issues associated with use of the pump. VA ECMO also poses the risk of left ventricular distention which results from the higher afterload produced by the pump.

stem: What is the most common type of cardiomyopathy among pediatric patients?
rationale: Cardiomyopathy is a disease of the myocardium. The heart muscle becomes abnormally thick, stiff, or enlarged, affecting the heart's ability to pump and maintain its rhythm. DCM is the most common type. The heart becomes enlarged and weakened; this condition has many causes but may be familial. Symptoms in infants include dyspnea, diaphoresis, poor weight gain, and irritability. Older children may have a heart murmur, fatigue, heart palpitations, dizziness, fainting, and dyspnea on exertion. Treatment includes diuretics, fluid restriction, inotropes, antiarrhythmic medications, a pacemaker, or a heart transplant.

stem: Which of the following statements regarding a sinusoidal fetal heart rate (FHR) pattern is most accurate?
options: sinusoidal FHR pattern may indicate anemia of the fetus
sinusoidal FHR pattern demonstrates less regularity in the shape and amplitude of wave variability but also maintains beat-to-beat variability
sinusoidal FHR pattern is frequently seen during tetanic uterine contractions
sinusoidal FHR pattern is a not uncommon finding on electronic fetal monitoring and resolves with repositioning of the mother ✗
answer: sinusoidal FHR pattern may indicate anemia of the fetus
rationale: A sinusoidal fetal heart rate (FHR) pattern is always a concerning finding and almost always indicates a need for emergency Cesarean section. A true sinusoidal FHR pattern is rare and can be recognized by regularity in shape and amplitude of FHR waves (a sine wave) and the absence of beat-to-beat variability. The FHR remains within normal range in sinusoidal pattern, and the pattern may look saw-toothed in appearance. Sinusoidal FHR may result from severe fetal anemia, acidosis of the fetus, hypovolemia of the fetus, accidental tapping of the umbilical cord during amniocentesis, or placental abruption. Sinusoidal FHR needs to be distinguished from pseudosinusoidal pattern which can result from the administration of labor drugs to the mother, or from the use of certain illicit substances.

stem: A medical transport team is providing transport for a toxic-appearing four-year-old girl with suspected epiglottitis. Which of the following statements regarding airway management for this patient is most accurate?
options: The medical transport crew should delay insertion of intravenous catheters until an anesthesiologist can establish a secure airway
The medical transport crew should insert two intravenous catheters to ensure adequate vascular access
The medical transport crew should closely monitor the patient and prepare to secure an airway using surgical cricothyroidotomy if her condition begins to deteriorate
The medical transport crew should closely monitor the patient and prepare to intubate the patient using rapid sequence intubation (RSI) if her condition begins to deteriorate ✗
answer: The medical transport crew should delay insertion of intravenous catheters until an anesthesiologist can establish a secure airway
rationale: Epiglottitis is a rare but deadly bacterial infection which affects the epiglottis, primarily in children between the ages of two and six years. The regular administration of the Haemophilus influenzae type b vaccine (Hib) has resulted in a significant decline in epiglottitis cases. Children with epiglottitis typically appear acutely ill and have a toxic appearance, and their caregivers typically report a rapid onset of symptoms of high fever, sore throat, inability to swallow their own secretions, and noisy breathing. During emergency medical transport, the child should be kept as calm and quiet as possible to prevent worsening of the edema of the epiglottis, supporting an upright, classic tripod position if at all possible during transport. The transport crew should not plan to insert any intravascular access or administer any other invasive procedures until either an anesthesiologist or ear/nose/throat doctor can secure an airway in the patient. Should the patient's condition worsen during transport, needle cricothyrotomy should be completed (surgical cricothyrotomy is not indicated in pediatric patients under the age of 10 to 12 years), and tracheotomy should be considered if a skilled provider is present.

stem: A four-month-old infant has been diagnosed with pertussis infection after exposure at her daycare. Which of the following represents the most commonly administered treatment for pertussis infection?
rationale: Infection with the Bordetella pertussis organism has increased dramatically in the United States (US) in the last 25 years despite adequate vaccination programs, possibly due to emerging strains of the organism. Pertussis, which is sometimes referred to as the "100-day cough," or more frequently, whooping cough, is spread through infected respiratory droplets, and in the US, most commonly affects infants, children between the ages of seven to 10 years, and adolescents. The most frequent presentation is prolonged coughing "fits" characterized by inspiratory stridor and the classic "whooping" sound giving its colloquial name. Infants who are infected with pertussis may be undiagnosed or misdiagnosed due to common coinfection with adenovirus or respiratory syncytial virus (RSV). It is not uncommon for infants and children with pertussis to vomit after the prolonged coughing paroxysms. Pertussis is most commonly treated with administration of azithromycin. Clarithromycin and erythromycin may also be used to treat pertussis. Dexamethasone may be administered at times to patients with pertussis to treat airway inflammation and reduce coughing. Amoxicillin is not indicated for treatment of pertussis infection.

stem: The medical air transport team is providing rapid transport for a post-dates pregnant female who was involved in an accident. During the transport, the patient's membranes rupture and the fluid is noted to be meconium-stained. Which of the following statements reflects the most current intervention practices recommended when infants are born meconium-stained?
options: Positive-pressure ventilation (PPV) should be administered to non-vigorous, meconium-stained neonates who have a heart rate below 100 beats/min
Immediately following delivery of the meconium-stained neonate, intubate the trachea and suction beneath the glottis
The neonate's head should be delivered and then the mouth and nose suctioned on the perineum prior to delivery of the shoulders
Tracheal intubation and suctioning of the glottis should only be administered to non-vigorous, meconium-stained neonates ✗
answer: Positive-pressure ventilation (PPV) should be administered to non-vigorous, meconium-stained neonates who have a heart rate below 100 beats/min
rationale: Fetuses who have experienced intrauterine stress may pass meconium prior to delivery, then presenting risk of meconium aspiration. Meconium aspiration syndrome can contribute to the development of respiratory distress, pneumonitis, pneumothorax, and persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn. There have been several practices changes made over the last 10 to 15 years in the management of the meconium-stained neonate. For several years, standard of practice in care of these infants was to deliver the head of the meconium-stained infant, and then suction the oro- and nasopharynx on the perineum prior to delivery of the shoulders. This practice was ultimately found not to prevent or improve meconium aspiration, and was discontinued. Following this, it became standard of care to provide tracheal intubation and deep suctioning of the non-vigorous, meconium-stained infant immediately following delivery. Vigorous, meconium-stained infants were monitored and standard newborn interventions were administered. More recently (2015), practice was changed again to reflect the most current recommendations on appropriate care of the meconium-stained infant: vigorous meconium-stained infants may stay with their mothers and receive standard newborn care; non-vigorous, meconium-stained infants should receive standard newborn resuscitation measures, namely the administration of positive-pressure ventilation (PPV), followed by the next consecutive steps of newborn resuscitation should the infant's condition not improve with administration of PPV. This most recent practice change was made to prevent delay in the administration of PPV, as previously the administration of tracheal intubation and suctioning had caused delay in delivery of mechanical ventilation.

stem: You are monitoring the fetal heart rate (FHR) of a 39-week gestation patient who is being transported for active labor. While watching the external fetal monitor (EFM) tracing, you note the ongoing presence of early decelerations in response to maternal uterine contractions. Which of the following interventions is the best option for the patients in this scenario?
options: Assist the maternal patient in moving onto her left side ✗
Continue monitoring of the FHR by EFM
Administer high-flow oxygen via nonrebreather mask to the maternal patient
Administer a crystalloid IV fluid bolus
answer: Continue monitoring of the FHR by EFM
rationale: Early decelerations of the fetal heart rate (FHR) as evidenced on external fetal monitoring (EFM) are non-pathologic findings which result from the pressure of the fetal head on the cervix during a contraction as a result of parasympathetic response of the vagal nerve. These decelerations are often observed during active labor when the cervix has dilated greater than 4 cm, and do not require any intervention on the part of the medical transport team.

stem: When do the anterior and posterior fontanelles close?
rationale: The posterior fontanelle closes several months before the anterior fontanelles close. The CFRN can use this knowledge to assess for conditions such as hydrocephaly or dehydration in children younger than 18 months.

stem: The medical transport team is transporting a recently delivered female patient who is experiencing amniotic fluid embolism/anaphylactic syndrome of pregnancy. All of the following treatment interventions should be implemented by the transport team, except:
options: Administration of hyperventilation to intubated patients
Administration of PEEP to intubated patients
answer: Administration of hyperventilation to intubated patients
rationale: Patients who have experienced amniotic fluid embolism/anaphylactic syndrome of pregnancy require supportive measures during transport due to the severity of the clinical picture. Most patients will require intubation, and PEEP (positive end-expiratory pressure) should be provided due to the high association with acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS). Multiple IV lines should be placed in order to deliver the necessary blood products and IV fluids to treat the complications of disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) associated with the disorder. Oxytocin in the amount of 20 to 40 units should be administered IV to treat uterine atony and hemorrhage (only administer oxytocin if the fetus has already been delivered). Narcotics like morphine and fentanyl can be administered to treat any pain, dyspnea, or anxiety.

stem: A 7-day-old infant is being transported via ambulance to the hospital for difficulty breathing and sudden onset of respiratory distress with cyanosis. You suspect a congenital heart defect (CHD). The infant's heart rate is 180 beats/minute, blood pressure is 45/27 mm Hg, respiratory rate is 70 breaths/minute, and capillary refill time is greater than 5 seconds. The infant requires intubation during transport for respiratory failure. Which of the following defects does this infant most likely have?
rationale: The ductus arteriosus is closed in a 7-day-old (typically closes 12-24 hours after delivery), which increases pulmonary blood flow while decreasing systemic perfusion. HLHS affects normal blood flow through the heart; the left side of the heart does not form correctly during gestation. The left ventricle is abnormally small, and there is aortic and mitral valve stenosis. The closed ductus arteriosus in an infant with HLHS causes severe cyanosis, hypoxia (that does not improve with intubation and mechanical ventilation), acidosis, and low cardiac output. TOF patients exhibit hypoxic spells that resolve when placing the baby in a knee-to-chest position. Patients with TGA will exhibit decreased pulmonary vascular resistance with oxygen administration, improving the mixing of systemic and venous blood (improving arterial oxygen saturation). Still, they will not show improvement in cyanosis with oxygen administration. Infants with coarctation of the aorta do not exhibit a sudden onset of respiratory distress.

stem: All of the following interventions are appropriate initial steps in the management of idiopathic persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN), except:
rationale: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN) is a syndrome in which the expected transition from fetal circulation to neonatal circulation fails to occur, resulting in right-to-left shunting of blood at the foramen ovale or the ductus arteriosus. This in turn results in significant pulmonary hypertension and hypoxemia. PPHN almost always occurs in term or near-term neonates who have experienced severe asphyxia or meconium aspiration syndrome, are septic at birth, or who have a congenital diaphragmatic hernia. Treatment of PPHN is aimed at decreasing the pulmonary vascular resistance, supporting adequate systemic blood pressure (higher than pulmonary pressures), and treating the hypoxemia, while at the same time minimizing or preventing barotrauma which so often results from the need for high ventilator pressures. 100% oxygen is often adequate initially, as it functions as a potent vasodilator. If the neonate is unable to maintain adequate oxygenation with the delivery of the 100% oxygen concentration, nitric oxide should be administered. Inotropic drugs, such as dopamine, should be administered to ensure adequate peripheral tissue perfusion and oxygenation, and high-frequency ventilation (oscillator, jet ventilator) should be used when possible if it is known the infant has parenchymal lung disease. Surfactant is not routinely used to treat PPHN unless the cause of the PPHN is known (in this scenario it was idiopathic), such as meconium aspiration syndrome, parenchymal lung disease, pneumonia, or sepsis.

stem: As part of a medical transport team, you are responsible for the care of a full-term newborn diagnosed with a congenital heart defect who is being transported to a tertiary care center for intervention. The infant is being maintained on a continuous prostaglandin E1 infusion to preserve ductus arteriosus patency. Which of the following congenital heart defects is most likely to benefit from maintenance of ductus arteriosus patency with a continuous prostaglandin E1 infusion?
rationale: Congenital heart defects that impair effective oxygenation or systemic perfusion during the transition from fetal to neonatal circulation may benefit from maintaining patency of the ductus arteriosus with prostaglandin E1. Transposition of the great arteries involves parallel rather than series circulation, making survival dependent on adequate mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood, which may occur at the atrial, ventricular, or ductal level. Maintaining ductus arteriosus patency can improve systemic oxygen delivery until definitive intervention is performed. Truncus arteriosus is characterized by a single outflow tract with inherent mixing and is not typically dependent on ductal flow for survival. Tetralogy of Fallot is not typically ductal-dependent unless pulmonary atresia is present. A ventricular septal defect (VSD) typically produces left-to-right shunting and does not require ductus arteriosus patency for survival.

stem: A 21-month-old toddler has been brought in for evaluation by his aunt who states she believes the child ingested a button battery. All of the following statements regarding button batteries are accurate, except:
options: Button batteries may release an alkaline substance that causes tissue necrosis
An ingested button may cause life-threatening complications if lodged in the esophagus
Ingested button batteries are not harmful if they pass unobstructed through the gastrointestinal tract
Button batteries are the most frequently aspirated of the foreign body items
answer: Button batteries are the most frequently aspirated of the foreign body items
rationale: While the incidence of foreign body battery ingestion has increased 80% in the last decade due to their more frequent prevalence in home-use electronic devices, the most commonly aspirated foreign body continues to be food products such as nuts and seeds. Button batteries are enticing to children because of their small size and shiny color and while, if they are swallowed, they are most likely to pass through the digestive tract without causing any problems, should the button battery become lodged in the esophagus (or in the nostril due to a child placing it there), it can cause potentially deadly complications. An impacted button battery generates an electrolytic current and begins releasing a toxic alkaline substance which causes liquefaction and necrosis of any tissues it comes into contact with. If an adult suspects a child has ingested or aspirated a button battery, they should immediately seek emergency medical treatment.

stem: Which of the following statements regarding breech presentation of the fetus is most accurate?
options: Breech presentation of the fetus is more likely in grand multiparity
Women who are over the age of 35 are more likely to have a fetus in breech presentation
The presence of oligohydramnios decreases the risk of breech presentation
The fetus is more likely to present in breech position in pregnancies \<= 36 weeks gestation ✗
answer: Breech presentation of the fetus is more likely in grand multiparity
rationale: Breech presentation of the fetus may present significant complication at time of delivery. The fetus may be positioned in any of several breech positions: with legs flexed on the abdomen and feet alongside the buttocks (complete breech presentation), with legs extended upward and feet near the head (frank breech presentation), with one foot presenting (footling breech presentation), or with a knee presenting (incomplete breech). Prior to 34 weeks gestation, the fetus is more likely to be positioned in a breech position due to the head being significantly larger than the rest of the body with the upper segment of the uterus providing more space for the larger fetal part. Breech presentation is more likely in women who are grand multiparas (have had more than 5 pregnancies past 20 weeks) or who are carrying more than one fetus; with polyhydramnios and oligohydramnios; placenta previa; and with uterine anomalies such as uterine tumors, septate uterus, and bicornuate uterus; or when there are fetal congenital anomalies, such as hydrocephalus.

stem: You are transporting a 38-year-old woman at 34 weeks gestation due to severe headache unrelieved by Tylenol. Patient reports fatigue, headache, and blurred vision. There is good fetal movement and she denies abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, or fluid leakage. Her pregnancy has been complicated by gestational hypertension, but previously she has not required any antihypertensive therapy. Vital signs are T. 98.7F, BP 170/110 mm Hg, and heart rate 80 bpm. Fetal monitoring shows a heart rate of 140 beats per minute, moderate variability, no accelerations, and no decelerations. Which of the following is the best next step in managing this patient?
rationale: The definition of preeclampsia has been refined as the presence of de novo hypertension (> 140 mm Hg systolic or > 90 mm Hg diastolic) after 20 weeks of gestation combined with proteinuria or other maternal organ dysfunction (renal, liver, neurologic). In the absence of proteinuria, thrombocytopenia, liver dysfunction, renal insufficiency, pulmonary edema, and cerebral dysfunction are criteria for preeclampsia. Preeclampsia is associated with intrauterine growth retardation, premature labor, low birth weight, abruptio placentae, and future risk of maternal cardiovascular disease. Headache, scintillating scotomata or other visual changes, abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, and decreased fetal movement require immediate reevaluation. Treat severe preeclampsia (blood pressure > 160 mm Hg) with antihypertensive agent (Labetalol, Hydralazine) and IV magnesium sulfate. Labetalol produces less hypotension and reflex tachycardia than hydralazine, though higher doses cause neonatal hypoglycemia, and long-term use is associated with fetal growth restriction. Hydralazine can lead to maternal hypotension and fetal distress, and requires waiting 20 minutes for a response between IV doses. Caution is advised against use of Nitroprusside during pregnancy, as there is a risk of fetal bradycardia. While there is no known risk of teratogenicity, there is a risk of cyanide toxicity and fetal death (based on animal data at 25 mcg/kg/min). Oxytocin generates uterine muscle contraction and is used for labor induction, postpartum hemorrhage, or as an adjunct to abortion.

stem: Infant/newborn seizures are most likely to result from all of the following, except:
rationale: Seizure activity in a newborn or very young infant almost always is indicative of a state of illness or injury and often presents as the first sign of central nervous system disorders. Most frequently, seizure activity in the newborn results from the presence of metabolic dysregulation (such as hypoglycemia), with opioid or other drug withdrawal, with intraventricular hemorrhage (preterm infants), infection, neonatal encephalopathy (often related to liver failure), and congenital brain abnormalities.

stem: What is the calculated GCS score for an 18-month old infant with a likely traumatic brain injury that displays spontaneous movement and eye-opening, but who cries inconsolably?
rationale: The Pediatric Glasgow Coma Scale score can be used to assess and track a patient's mental status and level of consciousness and has been shown to be especially accurate for babies and toddlers who needed acute intervention. It should be performed to establish a baseline after the primary assessment. Common applications include the need to consider intubation/definitive airway management in those with a GCS less than 8. However, the entire clinical picture must be taken into account. Like the standard GCS, the score alone is not sufficient to guide diagnosis or management. Any patient with an abnormal GCS warrants close assessment and monitoring.

stem: During chest compressions of a term parturient in cardiac arrest, at what rate should breaths be delivered by bag valve mask?
rationale: Per AHA 2020 ACLS guidelines, trained healthcare providers performing CPR for adults should use a 30:2 compression-to-rescue breath ratio; compressions should be at least 2 inches deep (allowing for complete chest recoil), and providers should switch roles every 2 minutes or 10 cycles, with minimal interruption, to reduce rescuer fatigue and ensure high-quality compressions.

stem: The medical transport team should implement all of the following interventions to promote developmental care when transporting a neonate, except:
options: Place a gel-filled pad under the neonate
Place the neonate in a transport isolette
Place the neonate in a blanket-roll nest ✗
answer: Place the neonate in a transport isolette
rationale: The neonate requiring medical transport is subject to a great number of stressors which can negatively affect the neonate's neurodevelopment. The transport team should take care to implement measures to protect the infant from these stressors, decreasing the potential of developing neurodevelopmental complications from exposure to these stressors. A gel pad can be placed under the infant during transport to minimize vibration and the effects of vibration on the body; the infant can be nested in blanket rolls or gel donuts to provide containment, positioning, and comfort, and earmuffs should be placed on the infant to reduce stressful noise exposure. The neonate is transported in an isolette for maintenance of thermoregulation.

stem: Which of the following statements best describes amniotic fluid embolism?
options: Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) resulting from the presence of an obstructing amniotic fluid embolus in the pulmonary vasculature
Obstruction of the pulmonary vasculature by amniotic fluid embolus ✗
Obstruction of the pulmonary vasculature by an embolus comprised of amniotic fluid, fetal cells, and lanugo hairs
Anaphylactic reaction to amniotic fluid and fetal cells
answer: Anaphylactic reaction to amniotic fluid and fetal cells
rationale: Amniotic fluid embolism has received a new nomenclature, due to what is now believed to be the etiology of this deadly pregnancy-related process. Anaphylactoid syndrome of pregnancy (or anaphylaxis syndrome of pregnancy) is believed to occur as a result of maternal anaphylactoid reaction to the presence of amniotic fluid and fetal cells within the maternal circulation as a result of labor and delivery. It is suspected that amniotic fluid and fetal cells enter the maternal circulation through the following three most likely routes: microscopic lacerations to the endocervical veins and to the lower uterine segment (occurring during cervical dilation), the placental attachment site (after delivery of the placenta), and the uterine veins (as a result of trauma). Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) occurs in conjunction with the anaphylaxis; the etiology of the DIC is unclear. Amniotic fluid embolism is much more likely to occur when there have been interventions during the labor, particularly the use of uterine stimulant medications to augment or induce labor, surgical delivery methods, or when uterine rupture occurs. Other risk factors include fetal demise, meconium in the amniotic fluid, placental abruption, large fetus, and precipitous delivery (among others).

stem: General impression of a three-year-old girl shows her to be alert with mild dyspnea on inspiration and pale skin color. Primary assessment reveals high-pitched inspiratory sounds (mild stridor) when agitated; otherwise, her breathing is quiet. SpO2 is 92% on room air, and she has mild intercostal retractions. Pulmonary auscultation shows adequate distal breath sounds bilaterally. Which is the most appropriate initial intervention for this child?
rationale: Croup (stridor) describes acute inflammatory diseases of the larynx, including viral croup (laryngotracheobronchitis), epiglottitis (supraglottitis), and bacterial tracheitis, although spasmodic croup, angioedema, laryngeal or esophageal foreign body, and retropharyngeal or peritonsillar abscess should be considered in a differential diagnosis as well. In typical cases, viral croup presents in children six months to five years of age in the fall and early winter, as symptoms of an upper respiratory tract illness, followed by a barking cough and stridor, in the absence of fever. Patients with mild disease may have stridor when agitated. As the obstruction worsens, stridor may occur at rest, accompanied in severe cases by retractions, air hunger, and cyanosis. On examination, the presence of cough and the absence of drooling favor the diagnosis of viral croup over epiglottitis. Treatment is based on symptoms and severity. Mild croup requires supportive therapy with oral hydration and minimal handling. Glucocorticoids (dexamethasone, 0.6 mg/kg intramuscularly as one dose) improves symptoms, reduces the duration of hospitalizations and frequency of intubations, and permits early discharge from the emergency department. A single dose of oral dexamethasone, given as soon as the clinical diagnosis of croup has been made, has been shown to reduce the clinical signs of croup within two hours, with further beneficial effects noted up to 10 hours following administration. Patients with stridor at rest require active intervention; in such cases, nebulized racemic epinephrine is commonly used because it has a rapid onset of action and is effective in alleviating symptoms and decreasing the need for intubation. Oxygen should be administered to patients with oxygen desaturation.

stem: Which of the following is most likely to result in persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN)?
rationale: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN) is a disorder of persistently elevated pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) resulting in a right-to-left shunting of blood, either at the foramen ovale or the ductus arteriosus. This causes the development of hypoxemia in the absence of a true cardiac defect. The symptoms of PPHN---respiratory distress, cyanosis, hypotension, inability to maintain adequate oxygen saturation---may easily be mistaken for those seen in infants with cyanotic heart defects, such as tetralogy of Fallot. PPHN is most frequently encountered in infants who have experienced meconium aspiration syndrome, severe birth asphyxia (as is seen in abruptio placentae), congenital diaphragmatic hernia, or sepsis.
